content:
# Leviticus Chapter 6 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)

**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 60r–60v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/LEV_006_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_006_page_002.jpg`

---

**8** The LORD spoke to Moses: **9** "Give Aaron and his sons these instructions: This is the procedure for the burnt offering. The burnt offering shall remain on the altar's hearth throughout the night until morning, with the altar fire kept burning underneath it. **10** The priest shall put on his linen robe and linen undergarments next to his skin, then take up the fatty ashes of the burnt offering that the fire has consumed on the altar, and deposit them beside the altar. **11** Then he shall change his clothes, put on other garments, and carry the ashes outside the camp to a ritually clean place. **12** The fire on the altar must be kept burning — it must never go out. The priest shall add wood to it every morning, arrange the burnt offering on it, and burn the fat portions of the peace offerings on it as well. **13** The fire must be kept continually burning on the altar; it must never go out.

**14** "This is the procedure for the grain offering: Aaron's sons are to present it before the LORD, in front of the altar. **15** The officiating priest shall take a handful of the fine flour of the grain offering along with some of the oil and all of the frankincense that is on it, and burn this token portion on the altar as a pleasing aroma to the LORD. **16** Whatever remains of it Aaron and his sons may eat. It is to be eaten without yeast, in the court of the tent of meeting — **17** it shall not be baked with yeast. I have given it as their share of my fire-offerings; it is most holy, like the purification offering and the guilt offering. **18** Every male among Aaron's descendants may eat of it. It is a permanent entitlement throughout your generations from the LORD's fire-offerings. Anyone who touches any of it must be holy."

**19** The LORD spoke to Moses: **20** "This is the offering that Aaron and his sons are each to bring to the LORD on the day he is anointed: a tenth of an ephah of fine flour as a regular grain offering, half of it in the morning and half in the evening. **21** It shall be prepared with oil on a griddle; bring it well mixed and offer the baked portions of the grain offering as a pleasing aroma to the LORD. **22** Whichever of Aaron's sons is the anointed priest who succeeds him shall prepare it. It is a permanent obligation — it is to be completely burned as an offering to the LORD. **23** Every grain offering of a priest shall be entirely burned; it may not be eaten."

**24** The LORD spoke to Moses: **25** "Tell Aaron and his sons: This is the procedure for the purification offering. The purification offering is to be slaughtered before the LORD in the same place where the burnt offering is slaughtered; it is most holy. **26** The priest who offers it for sin shall eat it — it is to be eaten in the court of the tent of meeting. **27** Whatever touches the flesh of the offering becomes holy; and if any of its blood spatters on a garment, that garment must be washed in a holy place. **28** The clay vessel in which the meat was boiled must be smashed; but if it was boiled in a bronze vessel, that vessel shall be scoured and rinsed in water. **29** Any male among the priests may eat it — it is most holy. **30** But no purification offering may be eaten when its blood has been brought into the tent of meeting to make atonement in the sanctuary; it must be burned in the fire."

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**The fire that must never go out — esh tamid.** The single most arresting phrase in the entire chapter appears twice, in verses 12 and 13, like a drumbeat: *lo tikhbeh* — "it shall not go out." The phrase *esh tamid* ("perpetual fire," or "fire of continuance") is more than a logistical instruction about fuel management. It is a theological statement about presence. The fire on the altar is not ordinary combustion. According to Leviticus 9:24 — which describes the moment the tabernacle is first dedicated — fire came out from before the LORD and consumed the burnt offering on the altar. This was not human fire. It was divine fire. The people who witnessed it fell on their faces. That originating flame was to be maintained from that day forward, passed on through careful attention, never allowed to die out. The perpetual fire is the continuing sign that the divine presence inhabits the altar, that the channel of communication between the human camp and the holy God has not been severed. When the fire goes out, it goes out for a reason — and the reason is catastrophic. Nadab and Abihu will shortly offer "strange fire" (*esh zarah*) before the LORD in chapter 10 and be consumed. Fire from God is blessing; fire from elsewhere, or the absence of fire altogether, is a rupture in the covenant relationship. The twice-repeated *lo tikhbeh* is the priest's solemn vow: we will not let this happen.

The morning ritual attached to the perpetual fire is also significant: the priest must *dress* the fire — he puts on sacred linen, lifts the accumulated fat-ash, and deposits it carefully beside the altar before changing into working clothes to carry it outside the camp. The ash is not simply discarded. It is given priestly attention — dressed in full ceremonial vestments, honored, placed in a clean location. Even the residue of the divine fire carries holiness. The contrast with a dying campfire being raked and scattered could not be more complete.

**Torah as instruction — the priestly operating manual.** Chapters 1–5 of Leviticus were addressed to the laity: "When any of you brings an offering..." Chapter 6 changes addressee and registers this change explicitly. The formula *tsav et-Aharon ve-et-banav* — "Command Aaron and his sons" — opens this chapter, distinguishing it from the earlier *daber el-benei Yisrael* ("Speak to the people of Israel"). What follows is a series of sections each headed by *zot torat ha-...* — "This is the torah of the..." This phrase is critical. *Torah* here is not the Torah as a canonical book. The word derives from the root *y-r-h*, "to instruct, to direct, to shoot straight," and it means simply *instruction* or *procedure* — a technical term for the detailed operating protocol of a given ritual. "This is the procedure for the burnt offering." "This is the procedure for the grain offering." "This is the procedure for the purification offering." Leviticus 7 will continue with the same heading for the guilt offering. Together these constitute a kind of priestly manual — the operational documentation for running the sacrifice system that the earlier chapters introduced from the worshipper's side. The congregation were told what to bring; now the priests are told what to do with it.

The elegance of this structural design is underappreciated. Chapters 1–5 are public-facing; chapters 6–7 are professional. The same ritual is described twice from two angles — a generous teaching strategy that ensures both parties to the transaction understand their respective responsibilities. Worship in Leviticus is a bilateral covenant performance: the worshipper brings; the priest acts; God receives. Both partners must know their part.

**The priest and the ash — dignity in the mundane.** Verses 10–11 describe a task that might seem inglorious: the priest scoops up the oily ash from the altar and hauls it outside the camp. But the text frames this task with full ceremonial attention. The priest dresses in his complete official linen ensemble — tunic, undergarments — to perform this task. Only after the ash is properly deposited beside the altar does he change into different clothes for the actual transport. Two changes of clothing for one ash-removal. The rabbis later called this *chafitsat mitzvah* — the zeal or eagerness with which sacred tasks are approached. No act of priestly service, however routine, is stripped of its dignity by being reduced to the merely functional. The fire is holy; the ash of the fire is holy; the removal of the ash is a holy act. You put on your best to do it.

This principle carries a weight well beyond the altar. It implies a theology of sacred work: that the holiness of an institution sanctifies all the labor associated with maintaining it, not merely the dramatic public moments. The stunning flame that came down from heaven in chapter 9 also produces ash that must be carried away. Both the fire and the ash belong to God.

**The grain offering — bread in the holy precincts.** Verses 14–18 establish the priests' portion of the grain offering (*minchah*). Every grain offering from the laity has two parts: the *azkarah* (the token portion burned on the altar) and the *sherit* (the remainder, which belongs to the priests). The remainder is to be eaten "without yeast, in the court of the tent of meeting" — two qualifications that together define the sacred meal. *Without yeast* links the grain offering to the purity logic of unleavened bread throughout the Torah: yeast is fermentation, which carries associations of decay, process, corruption of the pure thing. The priest's meal is to be plain, direct, unchanged from what God received. *In the court of the tent of meeting* establishes a sacred geography for priestly eating: the food is so holy that it cannot leave the sanctuary precincts. It must be consumed where it was offered. The place of offering and the place of eating are the same.

Verse 18 adds a striking provision: "Anyone who touches any of it must be holy." The verb is *yiqddash* — it shall become holy, or shall be made holy. The implication is that the grain offering carries a kind of contagious holiness: physical contact with a most-holy thing conveys holiness to the person or object that touches it. This is the "positive holiness contagion" principle, which will appear again in verse 27 for the purification offering. The logic is intuitive: the most holy things are charged with the divine presence, and that charge is transmissible. This is why such things must be kept away from ordinary people — not because ordinary people would contaminate the holy, but because the holy would overwhelm the ordinary. Contact with the most holy requires that the person be in a state of holiness to receive it without harm.

**The high priest's daily offering — the prayer he cannot eat.** Verses 19–23 describe what is perhaps the most theologically remarkable offering in Leviticus: the daily grain offering of the anointed high priest (*minchah tamid*). It is a tenth of an ephah of fine flour, half offered in the morning and half in the evening, prepared on a griddle with oil, offered as a pleasing aroma to the LORD. So far, it sounds like an ordinary grain offering. But verse 23 drops the structural bomb: *ve-chol-minchah le-kohen kalil tihyeh lo te'achel* — "Every grain offering of a priest shall be entirely burned; it may not be eaten."

Every grain offering of a priest is entirely burned. The priests eat the grain offerings of the laity; but the priest cannot eat his own grain offering. Why? The logic is embedded in the sacrificial principle throughout Leviticus: a person cannot benefit personally from an offering made on their own behalf. The offering is a gift directed from the offerer toward God. If the offerer eats what they have offered, the gift is circular — it never leaves, it never arrives, it is not a gift at all. For lay people, this principle is applied differently because the priests serve as mediators: the layperson's offering is received by the priest (who eats the remainder), which interrupts the circularity. But the priest has no mediator. There is no one between the priest and God to receive what the priest offers. So the high priest's grain offering must go entirely to the altar — entirely consumed, entirely surrendered, entirely God's.

This is a built-in structural reminder to the priesthood that they are not exempt from the fundamental logic of the sacrificial system. They are not above the altar; they serve the altar. Their daily offering burns entirely. They pray a prayer they cannot eat. The *minchah tamid* of the high priest is the most radically offered thing in the entire cultic system precisely because it is offered by the one who offers everything else.

**The purification offering — holy food, destructible vessels.** Verses 24–30 establish the procedures for the purification offering (*chattat*). Three details reveal the gravity of the offering's holiness.

First, the place: the purification offering must be slaughtered "in the same place where the burnt offering is slaughtered." This is the north side of the altar (established in 1:11), the spot of maximum ritual access and visibility. By placing the *chattat* slaughter in the same location as the *olah*, the text equalizes their gravity. The purification offering for sin is not a lesser sacrifice performed in a side corner; it is front and center, in the place of primary offering.

Second, the blood-stained garment must be washed "in a holy place" — not merely washed, but washed within the sanctuary precincts. This is the holiness-contagion principle again: the blood of the most holy offering is so charged with sacred significance that even accidental contact requires a response. The garment cannot simply be taken home and laundered in an ordinary washbasin. It must be processed within the holy sphere.

Third, and most strikingly: the clay vessel used to boil the meat must be smashed; the bronze vessel must be scoured and rinsed. Why the difference? The rabbinic tradition (and the logic of material science) explains it well: clay is porous. It absorbs. Once the most-holy flesh has been boiled in a clay pot, the holiness penetrates the clay matrix and cannot be fully extracted. The pot is permanently altered, permanently charged. Because the pot cannot be properly re-dedicated to ordinary use — and also cannot be used for a different offering — it must be destroyed. Bronze, however, is non-porous. It can be scoured clean, rinsed, and restored to a state of ritual neutrality. The difference in disposal methods is not arbitrary punctiliousness. It is a careful application of a principle about the nature of holiness and the nature of matter: some things, once touched by the most holy, are changed permanently; others can be cleansed.

**Verse 30 — the non-edible purification offering.** The chapter closes with a provision that seems to contradict what precedes it: normally the priests eat the purification offering (v. 26), but "no purification offering may be eaten when its blood has been brought into the tent of meeting to make atonement in the sanctuary; it must be burned in the fire." This exception applies to the offerings of chapters 4:5, 4:16, and 4:21 — the purification offerings made on behalf of the whole congregation or the high priest himself, where the officiating priest carries the blood inside the tabernacle and sprinkles it before the veil. In those cases, the priest (or high priest) is the offerer as well as the officiant. The same circularity principle applies: the one who offers cannot eat what is offered. When the blood enters the inner precincts on behalf of the priest himself, the priest's mediatorial role collapses — he becomes the supplicant, and the offering must be entirely burned, just as the high priest's own *minchah tamid* must be entirely burned. The two closing exceptions in chapter 6 — the high priest's grain offering and the inward-blood purification offering — share the same logic: when there is no mediator above you, your offering cannot return to you.

**LXX (Vaticanus) observations.** Vaticanus pages 90–91 (images pa_0090–0091) carry Leviticus 6 in the same three-column uncial hand. The Greek chapter division at this point follows a different versification scheme than the English and MT, and several translation choices illuminate the theology the Alexandrian translators heard in the text.

At verse 9 (LXX), the phrase *esh tamid* is rendered *πῦρ καυθήσεται ἐπὶ τοῦ θυσιαστηρίου* — "fire shall be kept burning upon the altar" — without an explicit equivalent for *tamid* in this first occurrence. But the second occurrence in verse 13 renders it more fully: *πῦρ διὰ παντὸς καυθήσεται ἐπὶ τοῦ θυσιαστηρίου, οὐ σβεσθήσεται* — "fire shall burn upon the altar continually (*dia pantos*); it shall not be extinguished." The Greek *dia pantos* ("through everything," i.e., unceasingly) is a strong equivalent for *tamid*, and *sbennymai* (to extinguish, to quench) translates *tikhbeh* precisely. Notably, *sbennymai* is the same verb Paul will use in 1 Thessalonians 5:19: *τὸ πνεῦμα μὴ σβέννυτε* — "do not quench the Spirit." The theological line connecting altar-fire to divine Spirit runs through this single Greek verb.

At verse 15, the *azkarah* (memorial/token portion) is rendered *μνημόσυνον* (*mnēmosynon* — "memorial, remembrance offering"). This is the standard LXX rendering of *azkarah* and reveals the Alexandrian theological interpretation: the token portion burned on the altar is specifically a *remembrance* — a prompt to divine attention, a calling to mind. The *mnēmosynon* language was heard in the synagogue tradition as a rich parallel to God "remembering" his covenant — the same verbal root (*zakar*) that underlies *azkarah* also underlies God "remembering" Noah (Genesis 8:1), "remembering" his covenant with Abraham (Exodus 2:24), and "remembering" Hannah (1 Samuel 1:19). The grain offering's burning portion is, on this reading, a small act of covenant memorial embedded in the daily liturgy.

At verse 20, the high priest's daily offering is described in the LXX as *θυσίαν αἰώνιον* (*thysian aiōnion* — "an eternal sacrifice/offering"). The word *aiōnion* — eternal, everlasting, of the age — is applied to the *minchah tamid* of the high priest, underlining the permanent, unconditional character of the obligation. The high priest who is anointed after Aaron will also perform this rite; the LXX makes the dynastic perpetuity explicit by adding the generational qualifier alongside the eternal one.

At verse 23 (the "entirely burned" provision), the LXX renders *kalil tihyeh* ("it shall be entirely burned, complete") as *ὁλοκαύτωμα* (*holokautōma*) — the same word used for the burnt offering, the *olah*. This is a significant exegetical move: the Alexandrian translators understood the entirely-burned grain offering of the priest as structurally equivalent to the *olah* of the laity. The high priest's daily grain offering is, functionally, his own burnt offering — his daily surrender of his own food-gift entirely to God, with nothing returning to himself. The semantic overlap of *kalil* and *holokautōma* quietly makes this equation explicit.

At verse 27, the holiness-contagion provision ("whoever touches the flesh becomes holy") is rendered *ἁγιασθήσεται* (*hagiasēthēsetai* — "shall be made holy, shall be sanctified"). The passive construction in Greek confirms the direction of the holiness transmission: it flows from the offering to the person, not from the person to the offering. The holy thing actively sanctifies; the person is the passive recipient of that sanctification. This is the theological grammar of Levitical holiness: holiness is not merely an achieved state but an experienced transmission, a contagion that flows from the holy source outward.

*— PNEUMA*
